items.title: Tune learning rate and n_estimators together
items.avoid: Fixing n_estimators=100 while only sweeping max_depth and ignoring η.
items.prefer: Grid or search over (learning_rate, n_estimators) pairs—lower η with more trees often generalizes better.
items.why: η and tree count are coupled: they jointly determine how much total correction the ensemble applies.
items.example: Try η ∈ {0.05, 0.1} with n_estimators ∈ {200, 400, 800} and pick by validation loss with early stopping.

items.title: Use early stopping on a validation set
items.avoid: Training the full n_estimators budget because training loss still decreases.
items.prefer: Monitor validation loss via staged predictions or library early_stopping_rounds; keep the best iteration.
items.why: Boosting continues to shrink training residuals even when validation performance degrades.
items.example: sklearn: `warm_start=True` with incremental `n_estimators` and break when val loss rises for 10 rounds.

items.title: Keep weak learners genuinely weak
items.avoid: Using max_depth > 10 hoping one round will solve the problem.
items.prefer: Start with max_depth 3–6; increase only if validation error remains high and learning curves look underfit.
items.why: Boosting theory assumes small steps from weak models; deep trees violate that assumption and amplify noise.
items.example: A depth-1 stump ensemble with η=0.1 can approximate smooth boundaries after hundreds of rounds on simple data.
